coredata.dc:title: Assembling g-C3N4 nanosheets on rod-like CoFe2O4 nanocrystals to boost photocatalytic degradation of ciprofloxacin with peroxymonosulfate activation

Liu, Yanan

Hong, Yuanzhi
Li, Xiangyu
Lin, Xue
Guo, Feng
Shi, Junyou
coredata.dc:description: Water pollution caused by organic pollutants seriously threatens the ecological balance. Photocatalytic oxidation technology with green, environment-friendly characteristics can effectively dispose of organic pollutants in water, is the potential replacement of the traditional sewage treatment technology. In this work, CoFe2O4/g-C3N4 heterojunction with nanosheets-on microrods structure was designed and prepared by a simple solvothermal-calcination synthetic method, which was applied for the degradation of cyprofloxacin (CIP) through the combination of peroxymonosulfate (PMS) and photocatalysis. The results reveal that the optimal photocatalytic degradation efficiency of CIP (20 mg/L) in the prepared CoFe2O4/g-C3N4-10/PMS system reached 97% within 60 min, and the degradation rate at a high level during the four cycles. The effects of different environmental factors (catalyst dose, PMS dose and initial CIP concentration) on the degradation of the system were studied. The enhancement of photocatalytic activity in the CoFe2O4/g-C3N4 composite can be mainly attributed to accelerated separation and transfer of electrons and holes through the formation of heterojunctions, which is beneficial to stimulate PMS to produce more SO4 •- and •OH.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Water pollution caused by organic pollutants seriously threatens the ecological balance. Photocatalytic oxidation technology with green, environment-friendly characteristics can effectively dispose of organic pollutants in water, is the potential replacement of the traditional sewage treatment technology. In this work, CoFe2O4/g-C3N4 heterojunction with nanosheets-on microrods structure was designed and prepared by a simple solvothermal-calcination synthetic method, which was applied for the degradation of cyprofloxacin (CIP) through the combination of peroxymonosulfate (PMS) and photocatalysis. The results reveal that the optimal photocatalytic degradation efficiency of CIP (20 mg/L) in the prepared CoFe2O4/g-C3N4-10/PMS system reached 97% within 60 min, and the degradation rate at a high level during the four cycles. The effects of different environmental factors (catalyst dose, PMS dose and initial CIP concentration) on the degradation of the system were studied. The enhancement of photocatalytic activity in the CoFe2O4/g-C3N4 composite can be mainly attributed to accelerated separation and transfer of electrons and holes through the formation of heterojunctions, which is beneficial to stimulate PMS to produce more SO4 •- and •OH.

ce:section.ce:para: Ciprofloxacin (CIP) is a new kind of broad-spectrum antibacterial drug, which has strong permeability, strong and rapid bactericidal power, and is widely used in medicine and other fields [1–3]. However, due to the abuse of CIP and improper treatment of animal manure, drug waste and expired drugs, CIP enters into the water environment and causes water pollution [4,5]. Unfortunately, traditional wastewater treatment techniques such as physical, biological and chemical oxidation methods do not satisfy sewage treatment efficiency as well as environment friendly requirements, causing drug residues to go into the water environment of biological survival and then endanger the entire ecosystem [6–8]. Consequently, the urgent need for a superior and green environment friendly technology to replace traditional technology to remove CIP from the water environment.

Photocatalysis technique has achieved remarkable results in the treatment of water pollution due to its low cost, mild operating conditions and energy saving [9–18]. Nevertheless, common photocatalytic technology itself still suffers from the unsatisfactory efficiency for CIP degradation. Fortunately, peroxymonosulfate (PMS)-based activated photocatalytic technology has proven to be effective strategies to significantly improve antibiotic degradation efficiency compared to common photocatalytic reactions [19–22]. There are two reasons which may explain this enhancement of activity: (i) PMS can effectively capture electrons and accelerate the transfer and separation of photo-generated charges [23,24]; (ii) PMS can be activated by photo-induced electrons and holes to produce SO4 •- radicals with strong oxidation ability [25,26]. Thus, the construction of effective catalyst for the removal of antibiotic pollutant though PMS-assisted photocatalytic technology with producing multiple reactive oxygen (ROSs) has the potential applied value in the field of water pollution treatment.

Recently, graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) has attracted wide attention as a visible and non-toxic material due to its low cost, low toxicity and convenience in synthesis [27–30]. Although g-C3N4 exhibits several advantages, the high recombination rate, the visible-light photocatalytic performance of pristine g-C3N4 is weak due to the poor absorption in the range of visible light and easily recombination of photogenerated electron-hole pairs [31–34], which limits its further development.

Popularly, combined with other semiconductor photocatalysts to form heterojunction can effectively promote the transfer and separation of photo-induced charges, thus improving the photocatalytic performance [35–39]. Previous reports have confirmed that cobalt ferrite (CoFe2O4) with several merits, including low cost, narrow band gap, good photochemical stability and strong visible light response, can form efficient heterojunctions with g-C3N4 due to the energy level matching, which can not only promote rapid separation of charge but also effectively enhance visible light absorption of composite materials for enhancing the photocatalytic activity [25,40–43]. To date, however, it has rarely been reported about the g-C3N4 coupling to CoFe2O4 for the photocatalytic degradation of CIP with PMS assistance and studying the corresponding mechanism of generated active species in the synergistic catalytic reaction. In addition, transition metal ions (Fe, Co) in CoFe2O4 can further accelerate the activation of PMS to produce SO4 •- active species. Meanwhile, its inherent magnetism can encourage composite materials to easily recover and reuse in water bodies [44]. Consequently, combine with two semiconductor materials of CoFe2O4 and g-C3N4 to form heterogeneous junctions may provide the satisfactory removal efficiency for the photocatalytic degradation of CIP based on PMS activation.

In this work, CoFe2O4/g-C3N4 heterojunction with nanosheets-on-microrods structure was constructed for the photocatalytic degradation of CIP based on PMS activation under visible light irradiation. In addition, the effects of catalyst dosage, pollutant concentration and PMS concentration on the degradation were investigated. The main active species produced in the process of photodegradation were analyzed. Finally, the synergistic effect of CoFe2O4/g-C3N4 on PMS activation and removal mechanism of CIP were speculated.

The synthetic method of g-C3N4 nanosheets was based on the previous reported literature [45]. Specifically, 10 g of urea powder was put into a covered alumina crucible, heated to 550 °C at 0.5 °C/min in the furnace, and maintained at this temperature for 3 h. CoFe2O4/g-C3N4 composites were prepared by a simple solvothermal method followed the Scheme 1. In a typical process, different proportions of g-C3N4, 1 mmol Fe(acac)3 and 0.5 mmol Co(NO3)2.6H2O were added to a mixed solvent containing 25 mL DMF and 15 mL ethanol, and then 1.67 mmol terephthalic acid was added above solution and strongly stirred for 2 h. Subsequently, the mixed solution was stirred for 30 min, then transferred to a 50 mL autoclave and kept at 120 ℃ for 12 h. After cooling to room temperature, the solid products were washed with ethanol for three times and dried at 65 ℃ for 6 h in an oven. Finally, the dried solid products were put into a muffle furnace and reacted at 400 ℃ for 1 h at the rate of 1 ℃/min to obtain the final composite samples. Based on this, various mass ratios of CoFe2O4/g-C3N4 composites at 5 wt%, 7.5 wt%, 10 wt% and 12.5 wt% were fabricated and denoted as CoFe2O4/g-C3N4-X (X = 5, 7.5, 10 and 12.5). The synthetic steps of pure CoFe2O4 is similar to the above process in the absence of g-C3N4 powder.

The photocatalytic degradation experiments of CIP were carried out to evaluate the degradation performance of various photocatalysts. Specifically, 25 mg of as-prepared photocatalyst was added into 20 mg/L ciprofloxacin (CIP) solution and dark adsorption process was carried out for 30 min to reach adsorption-desorption equilibrium. Then, 0.1 mmol peroxymonosulfate (PMS) was added to the above solution and the 500 W Xe lamp with UV cut-off filter (λ > 420 nm) light was turned on at the same time. During the light reaction process, 3 mL of reaction liquid was taken at regular time intervals, centrifuged at 5000 rpm and the supernatant was collected to determine the concentration of CIP by the UV-Vis spectrophotometry. The detail characterizations, active species capturing tests were given in the Supporting Information.

The crystalline phase and structure of as-prepared samples were confirmed by using X-ray diffraction (XRD, Fig. 1a). As can be seen the CoFe2O4 sample, all the distinct diffraction peaks at 18.1°, 30.2°, 35.6°, 43.3°, 53.6°, 57.2° and 62.7°of CoFe2O4 can be ascribed to the planes of (111), (220), (311), (400), (422), (511) and (440) of spinel CoFe2O4 (JCPDS No.22–1086), respectively [46]. In g-C3N4 sample, it can be seen that there are two typical diffraction peaks, which are located at 13.0°and 27.5°, attributing to (100) and (002) crystallographic planes, respectively [47–50]. However, when the content of g-C3N4 was low, no obvious characteristic peak of g-C3N4 was observed in the composite CoFe2O4/g-C3N4, which may be attributed to the low content of g-C3N4 in the composite material [51]. With the increase of g-C3N4 content, the (002) characteristic peak of g-C3N4 can be obviously observed in the CoFe2O4/g-C3N4 composite, which proves that the two semiconductors in the composite are successfully combined. And no other characteristic peaks were observed in the spectrum, indicating that the purity of the prepared sample was high. The successful preparation of the composite can be further proved by the following FT-IR spectra (Fig. 1b). For pure CoFe2O4, the absorption bands at 570 cm−1 can be assigned to the stretching vibration of Fe–O bond [40]. In the sample of g-C3N4, the peaks of 1200–1700 cm−1 are considered to be the stretching of C-N heterocycles, and the peaks located at 3000–3500 cm −1 are usually ascribed to the vibrational mode of O-H and N-H bonds [52–55]. In addition, the peak at 890 cm−1 is due to the deformation mode of N-H, and 808 cm−1 is the stretching vibration of triazine system [56–58]. All the characteristic peaks of CoFe2O4 and g-C3N4 exist in the CoFe2O4/g-C3N4-10 composite, which confirms that the successful combination of two semiconductors.

The microstructure and morphology of the as-synthesized samples were tested by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and transmission electron microscope (TEM). Fig. 2a displays that the as-prepared CoFe2O4 in this work possesses a rod structure with smooth surface and good dispersion. As can be seen in Fig. 2b, the g-C3N4 shows lamellar structure and has obvious agglomeration and accumulation phenomenon. After coating g-C3N4, the surface of rod structure of CoFe2O4 becomes rough, which is help for reducing the agglomeration of g-C3N4 (Fig. 2c). TEM image of g-C3N4/CoFe2O4-10 composite in Fig. 2d shows that the rod-like CoFe2O4 with the size of about 150 nm is composed of massive nanocrystals and the edge of rod-like CoFe2O4 is loaded with g-C3N4 nanosheets. HRTEM image in Fig. 2e shows that the lattice spacing of CoFe2O4 is 0.251 nm, which matches well with the (311) crystal plane of CoFe2O4 [59]. Meanwhile, the elemental mapping of the sample in Fig. 2f clearly shows the distribution of C, N, O, Co and Fe elements in the composite, which further proves the successful fabrication of CoFe2O4/g-C3N4-10 photocatalyst.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were measured to further understand the elemental composition and valence state of g-C3N4, CoFe2O4 and CoFe2O4/g-C3N4-10. Fig. 3a shows the survey of as-prepared samples. It can be seen that the main elements in the composite are composed of Co, Fe, O, C and N, which proves the successful preparation of the CoFe2O4/g-C3N4-10 heterojunction. Fig. 3b exhibits the Co 2p spectra of CoFe2O4 and CoFe2O4/g-C3N4-10. There are two distinct characteristic peaks at 780.4 eV and 795.8 eV with their satellite features, which belong to Co 2p3/2 and Co 2p1/2, respectively [60]. In the spectrum of Fe 2p (Fig. 3c), two main peaks located at 711.0 eV and 724.8 eV with their satellite features are attributed to Fe 2p3/2 and Fe 2p1/2, proving that Fe exists as Fe3+ in CoFe2O4 [61]. The O 1 s spectra of CoFe2O4 and CoFe2O4/g-C3N4-10 are displayed in Fig. 3d. It can be observed that there are three distinct characteristic peaks at 529.9, 531.6 and 533.3 eV, which are attributed to the lattice oxygen, the surface hydroxyl and the lattice oxygen in chemically adsorbed H2O in CoFe2O4, respectively [62]. The C 1 s spectra of g-C3N4 and CoFe2O4/g-C3N4-10 are shown in Fig. 3e. The characteristic peak at 287.9 eV in pure g-C3N4 is attributed to C-N-C coordination [63–65]. The characteristic peak at 284.5 eV is attributed to the amorphous carbon on the surface of g-C3N4 [66,67]. In the N 1s spectrum (Fig. 3f), the main peak at 398.4 eV is attributed to sp2-hybridized pyridine N nitrogen (CN-C) [68,69]. In addition, the characteristic high-resolution XPS peaks in the CoFe2O4/g-C3N4-10 composite shifts relative to the pure CoFe2O4 and g-C3N4, which may be due to the interaction between CoFe2O4 and g-C3N4.

The optical absorption properties of CoFe2O4, g-C3N4 and CoFe2O4/g-C3N4-10 heterojunction were investigated. As exhibited in Fig. 4a, the pure CoFe2O4 has a broad optical absorption in the range of 200–800 nm, which is attributed to the narrow band gap of CoFe2O4. And g-C3N4 displays an absorption edge at around 470 nm. In CoFe2O4/g-C3N4-10 composite, the introduction of CoFe2O4 significantly increases the light absorption intensity in the visible region compared with the pure g-C3N4 (the digital photographs of all samples shown in Fig. S1. Fig. 4b presents the Tauc's plots corresponding to (αhv)2 versus (hv) of CoFe2O4, g-C3N4 and CoFe2O4/g-C3N4-10 composite. Based on Kubela-Munk function, the band gaps of CoFe2O4, g-C3N4 and CoFe2O4/g-C3N4-10 are calculated to be about 1.38, 2.7 and 2.3 eV, respectively.

The photocatalytic activity of CoFe2O4/g-C3N4 was evaluated by activating PMS for degrading CIP under visible light irradiation. Firstly, the control experiments were carried out under different conditions for the degradation of CIP, as shown in Fig. 5a. When only light conditions exist, the degradation performance of CIP over CoFe2O4 and g-C3N4 can be ignored, while CoFe2O4/g-C3N4-10 exhibits a degradation efficiency of 56%, which may be attributed to the synergistic effect between CoFe2O4 and g-C3N4. In the dark, the degradation CIP effect of g-C3N4 activated PMS is almost negligible. Notably, the degradation efficiency of CIP by pristine CoFe2O4 activation PMS was 68%, which may be due to the important role of transition metal ions (Co and Fe ions) of CoFe2O4 in the activation PMS process [70]. The effect of accelerating the transfer of charge through heterojunction can further explain that degradation CIP activity of CoFe2O4/g-C3N4-10 can reach 84% in the presence of PMS. Notably, degradation efficiency of CIP was significantly enhanced in the coexist of PMS and light, and the degradation efficiency of CIP over CoFe2O4/g-C3N4-10 can be reached to 97% during 60 min irradiation. It can be seen from the UV-Vis spectrum of CIP degradation in Fig. 5b that the characteristic absorption spectrum of CIP disappeared after 60 min of visible light irradiation. The results of the control experiment reveal that CoFe2O4/g-C3N4-10 heterojunction can be activated PMS for degradation of CIP through the generated synergy effect during the photocatalytic process.
Furthermore, the effects of g-C3N4 loading content, initial CIP concentration, catalyst dosage, PMS concentration, initial pH and anions on photocatalytic CIP degradation were also investigated.

As exhibited in Fig. 6a, the photocatalytic performance of CoFe2O4/g-C3N4 with different loading g-C3N4 contents demonstrated the optimal degradation efficiency of CIP can reach 97% by the CoFe2O4/g-C3N4-10 sample, which is mainly due to the formation of the heterojunction interface between CoFe2O4 and g-C3N4, facilitating the efficient transfer of the charge [71]. In Fig. 6b, the effect of initial CIP concentrations on degradation in this system was observed under the same conditions, showing that the higher the concentration of CIP, the worse the degradation effect is, which is due to the high reactant concentration occupying the adsorption and active sites of the material, causing weakened activity [72]. Fig. 6c presents the photocatalytic performance of CIP degradation by changing the catalyst dosages. It can be seen that with the increase of catalyst content, the degradation efficiency increases, reaching the highest at 25 mg, but continues to increase to 45 mg, the degradation efficiency begins to decline, which may be ascribed to the fact that catalyst covers too many active sites, affecting the degradation activity [73]. The photocatalytic degradation effect of the present system was observed by changing the concentration of PMS (Fig. 6d). It can be seen that the degradation effect increases with the increase of PMS concentration (less than 0.2 M), but when the PMS concentration increases to 0.2 M, the degradation effect does not continue to increase, which may be that the limited number of photocarriers produced by the photocatalyst cannot satisfy the activation excess of PMS [74]. Catalytic stability is an important indicator of evaluating the catalyst. As shown in the Fig. 6e, the photodegradation activity of CoFe2O4/g-C3N4-10 still achieve outstanding performance in the in the four-cycle experiments, indicating that the catalyst possess good stability. Meanwhile, the XRD patterns and FT-IR spectra in Figs. 6f and S2 before and after the photocatalytic reaction reveals that the crystal structure of the as-synthesized CoFe2O4/g-C3N4 heterojunction still maintains the original status. The inset in Fig. 6f is a magnetic recovery picture of the sample, which can be seen that the catalyst is easily separated from the solution by an external magnetic field, showing good magnetic recovery performance.

The transient photocurrent response curves and electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) of the as-prepared samples were tested [75–77]. As shown in Fig. 7a, it is obvious that as-prepared samples exhibit transient photocurrent responses and photostability under visible light irradiation. Significantly, the photocurrent intensity of as-prepared CoFe2O4/g-C3N4 composite is higher than that of the pure CoFe2O4 and g-C3N4, indicating that the introduction of g-C3N4 could enhance the separation and transfer efficiency of the photogenerated charge carriers of CoFe2O4. In addition, the EIS plots in Fig. 7b also display that the impedance radius of CoFe2O4/g-C3N4 heterojunction is smaller than that of CoFe2O4 and g-C3N4, proving that photogenerated electrons-holes pairs can be efficiently separated by loading g-C3N4 nanosheets onto the CoFe2O4 microrods.

To investigate the effect on the separation efficiency of electron-hole pairs of as-prepared samples, photoluminescence (PL) spectra tests were performed. As exhibited in Fig. 8a, it is observed that a significantly weakened emission peak at near 480 nm in the CoFe2O4/g-C3N4-10 in comparison of g-C3N4, indicating that the construction of heterojunctions could inhibit the recombination of photogenerated carriers [78]. Moreover, Figs. S4 and 8b present the time-resolved transient photoluminescence decay spectra of CoFe2O4, g-C3N4 and CoFe2O4/g-C3N4-10. According to the corresponding formula [79]: τave= A1 * τ1 + A2 * τ2, τave represents the average fluorescence lifetime, τ1 and τ2 represent the fast and slow components, A1 and A2 represent the proportion of τ1 and τ2 in the fluorescence lifetime. It can be calculated that the average fluorescence lifetimes of CoFe2O4, g-C3N4 and CoFe2O4/g-C3N4-10 are 24.08, 36.5 and 43.7 ns, respectively, which demonstrates that covering g-C3N4 nanosheets on CoFe2O4 microrods surface can effectively improve the carrier life of composite material and effectively promote photocatalytic activity [80].

In order to determine the radical types of active species played during the photocatalytic degradation of CIP, free radical scavenging experiments were carried out. As exhibited in Fig. 9a and b, Tert-butyl alcohol (TBA) and ethanol (EtOH) are corresponded to •OH and SO4 •-, respectively. It can be seen that both of them have inhibitory effect on the degradation experiment, indicating that both of them play important roles in the reaction process. In addition, to further confirm the existence of •OH and SO4 •- during the degradation of CIP, an electron spin resonance (ESR) self-selective trapping experiment was tested, and 5,5-Dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) was used as a free radical spin trapping agents [81–84]. As exhibited in Fig. 9c and d, no signal peaks were observed under dark conditions, indicating that as-prepared sample could not produce any active species without light irradiation, indicating that the catalyst involved in was indeed photocatalytic reaction. Meanwhile, it also proves that the PMS could not be activated under dark condition [85]. After 10 min of light exposure, obvious characteristic peaks of SO4 •- and •OH appeared in the CoFe2O4/g-C3N4-10/PMS system, which fully proves that the introduction of PMS in the degradation process can increase the production of SO4 •- and •OH, thereby improving the synergistic effect of photocatalysis and persulfate-based advanced oxidation process.

In order to study the photocatalytic mechanism of CoFe2O4/g-C3N4 heterojunction, the band positions of CoFe2O4 and g-C3N4 were determined by the valance band XPS (VB-XPS) spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. S3, the VB positions of CoFe2O4 and g-C3N4 are 1.96 eV and 1.65 eV, respectively. According to the formula E
g
=E
VB
-E
CB , it can be calculated that the CB positions of CoFe2O4 and g-C3N4 are 0.58 eV and − 1.05 eV, respectively. Based on the above obtained results, the possible photocatalytic mechanism of the CoFe2O4/g-C3N4/PMS system heterojunction was proposed in Fig. 10. Under visible light irradiation, both CoFe2O4 and g-C3N4 can be excited to generate photo-generated electron-hole pairs. Furthermore, electrons were excited from the VBs of CoFe2O4 and g-C3N4 to their respective CBs, and holes remain in the VBs of CoFe2O4 and g-C3N4. Since the CB of g-C3N4 is more negative than the potential of O2/O2 •- (−0.33 eV) [86], some photogenerated electrons in the CB of g-C3N4 can react with O2 to form O2 •-, and O2 •- further reacts with H2O to form •OH (Eqs. (1)–(2)) [87]. Meanwhile, since the CB of g-C3N4 is more negative than the CB of CoFe2O4, the photogenerated electrons in CB of g-C3N4 are transferred to the CB of CoFe2O4, and further activate PMS to produce SO4 •- [20]. Similarly, compared with g-C3N4, the holes produced by VB in CoFe2O4 are transferred to the VB of g-C3N4, and the holes combine with PMS to generate SO5 •-, and further generate SO4 •- [40]. In addition, SO4 •- can react with H2O or OH- to generate •OH (Eqs. (3)–(6)) [88]. In this way, the separation of photogenerated carriers can be effectively promoted, more electrons can be advanced to participate in the reaction for improving the photocatalytic activity. Therefore, through the main active substances SO4 •- and •OH play an excellent catalytic role in the CoFe2O4/g-C3N4/PMS system, the pollutants are finally degraded into CO2 and H2O.
(1) e- + O2 → •O2 -
(2) •O2 - + 2H2O → 2 OH- + 2 •OH
(3) h + + HSO5 - → SO5 •- + H+
(4) 2 SO5 •- → 2 SO4 •- + O2
(5) SO4 •- + OH- → •OH + SO4 2-
(6) SO4 •- + H2O → •OH + SO4 2- + H+

In conclusion, CoFe2O4/g-C3N4 heterojunction with nanosheets-on microrods structure were prepared by a facile solvothermal-calcination route. The as-prepared CoFe2O4/g-C3N4 heterojunction exhibits higher photocatalytic degradation rate of CIP (97% within 60 min) within the activation of PMS and magnetic-recyclable capability. Stability experiments also demonstrate that the CoFe2O4/g-C3N4 heterojunction shows strong degradation ability after 4 cycles. This improved degradation performance is mainly due to the synergistic effect between CoFe2O4 and g-C3N4 by forming the heterojunction, which reduces the recombination rate of electron hole pairs and extends carriers life, improving the photocatalytic activity. Free-radicals capturing and ESR experiments also verify that two very powerful active species (SO4 •- and •OH) play a major role. Our work can pave the way for fabricating more efficient heterojunction based on the combination of PMS and photocatalysis technology to meet the demand of environment for the better future development.
